flashcards.front: What is file system mounting in operating systems?
flashcards.back: File system mounting is the process by which the operating system makes a file system (on a storage device) accessible at a specific directory location, called a mount point, integrating it into the existing directory tree structure.

A mount point is an existing directory in the file system hierarchy where a new file system will be attached. This directory becomes the root of the newly mounted file system, and accessing it provides access to the mounted device's contents.

flashcards.front: What is the basic syntax of the mount command in Linux?
flashcards.back: The basic syntax is: mount -t type device dir [-o options]. This command tells the kernel to attach the file system found on device (of type type) at the directory dir with specified options.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of /etc/fstab file?
flashcards.back: /etc/fstab (file systems table) is a configuration file that defines how disk partitions and remote file systems should be automatically mounted at boot time. Each line contains: device, mount point, file system type, options, dump, and fsck order.

flashcards.front: What is unmounting and why is it important?
flashcards.back: Unmounting is the process of detaching a mounted file system from the directory tree. It is crucial because it ensures all pending writes are completed and data integrity is maintained. Failing to unmount can lead to data loss or corruption.

A bind mount allows mounting a directory or file to another location within the file system. The same content is accessible at both paths. It is created using: mount --bind olddir newdir or mount -o bind olddir newdir.

flashcards.front: What is a loop device in Linux?
flashcards.back: A loop device is a pseudo-device that allows a regular file to be accessed as a block device. This enables mounting ISO images or creating file-backed virtual disks without needing actual physical storage devices.

Lazy unmount (umount -l) detaches the file system from the hierarchy immediately but cleans up all references to the file system only when it is no longer in use. It is useful for unresponsive or unavailable remote file systems.

flashcards.front: What does the mount option 'ro' and 'rw' mean?
flashcards.back: 'ro' mounts the file system as read-only, preventing any write operations. 'rw' mounts the file system as read-write, allowing both read and write operations. These options control access permissions for the mounted file system.

flashcards.front: What is the nosuid mount option?
flashcards.back: The nosuid option prevents the execution of set-user-identifier or set-group-identifier programs (SUID/SGID) on the mounted file system. This is a security measure to avoid potential privilege escalation attacks.

Auto mounting is a mechanism where file systems are automatically mounted when accessed, rather than being permanently mounted. The automount daemon (autofs) watches certain directories and mounts requested file systems on demand after a specified timeout.

flashcards.front: What is a virtual file system (VFS)?
flashcards.back: Virtual File System (VFS) is an abstraction layer in the kernel that provides a uniform interface for different file system types. It allows user programs to access files without knowing the underlying file system implementation (ext4, NTFS, NFS, etc.).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between hard mount and soft mount in network file systems?
flashcards.hard: Hard mount keeps retrying indefinitely if the server is unreachable, causing processes to hang until the mount becomes available. Soft mount returns an error after a timeout, allowing processes to continue but potentially causing data inconsistencies.

flashcards.back: Mount namespaces (introduced in Linux 2.6.15) provide isolation between processes, allowing each namespace to have its own set of mount points. This enables containers to have their own private file system views.
